data.title: Cyient Semiconductors’ Power Play: How Kinetic Technologies Fits the Puzzle
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Cyient Semiconductors, a Hyderabad, India-based fabless custom ASIC firm, has pursued a series of strategic partnerships and acquisitions since its official launch in April 2025, including the acquisition of Kinetic Technologies. About a year after spinning off from parent company Cyient to focus exclusively on semiconductors, it has taped out more than 25 ASICs and shipped millions of units.

Cyient Semiconductors has sealed several ecosystem partnerships, including (but not limited to):

  • Co-development with Navitas on GaN products for the Indian market
  • Co-development with MIPS for custom RISC-V-based ASICs
  • Channel partnership with GlobalFoundries as an authorized reseller
  • Partnership with Anora to build a semiconductor validation and production test facility
  • Majority stake acquisition in Kinetic Technologies

These moves reflect a larger scheme to position the company—India’s largest custom silicon provider—in intelligent, efficient silicon power conversion solutions. A recent conversation with Suman Narayan, CEO of Cyient Semiconductors, outlined the rationale behind the approach: “We’re building a domain-optimized intelligent power platform that scales across applications.”

In other words, the ASIC company is expanding its reach and expertise in four key blocks—MCU/CPU, power, analog mixed-signal, and RF—with a particular recent focus on power.

The full interview can be viewed below:

Majority stake in Kinetic

Perhaps the most striking of all these moves is the acquisition of a majority stake in Kinetic Technologies for $85 million, down from the $93 million originally announced. With 100+ patents and 250 application-specific standard products (ASSPs) focused on power management IP, Kinetic brings a strong portfolio in power conversion. Kinetic generates tens of millions in revenue annually, with a total of 3 billion parts sold under its port protection solutions alone.

“It really positions us as a custom ASSP company primarily around DC-to-DC conversion on the low-voltage side,” said Narayan in a separate, recent interview focused on the Kinetic closing. The deal potentially doubles their serviceable addressable market to $8.5 billion, targeting both low- and high-voltage segments.

Kinetic Technologies is a nearly 20-year-old analog and mixed-signal company in San Jose, California, with merchant silicon spanning surge-protection ICs, USB data-line protection ICs, motor-control ICs, EMI/ESD suppression, DC/DC conversion solutions, and more. At first glance, the company seems to follow a more traditional catalog ASSP model that diverges slightly from Cyient Semiconductors’ custom ASIC model.

However, Narayan sees it as a custom-ASSP play. “They know the market, and they’re building custom chips for that market,” he said. Ultimately, he sees the unison as a method to tighten three key aspects of building any custom ASSP or ASIC for both companies: reducing BOM costs, increasing product integration, and improving power efficiency.

“The customer wants IP integrated, and we’re happy to do that integration,” said Narayan, elaborating on the reasoning behind the majority Kinetic stake. “We’re still straddling the question of where the ASICs go across multiple markets, while the ASSPs go after a fixed-power market—in this case, the low-voltage power-protection market.”

The Kinetic Technologies team can draw on engineering talent from AnSem, acquired by parent company Cyient in 2018 and folded into Cyient Semiconductors at spinoff. The AnSem team has deep roots at imec, enabling Cyient Semiconductors to build on its mixed-signal expertise with the Leuven, Belgium-based design center.

The expanded talent pool could strengthen Kinetic’s operations by adding an agile analog and power team. Narayan continued, “It’s first pass silicon or bust for them [Kinetic]; they’re a good machine, and we can now help with some of the engineering prowess.” Narayan added that Cyient Semiconductors was also providing access to their sales team and connections with large distributors. “We don’t want to disturb the DNA [of Kinetic]. We want to enhance it.”

Access to an expanded customer base is equally significant, giving Cyient Semiconductors a foothold in regions where it had no prior presence and a path into Asian OEM supply chains through Kinetic’s decade-long relationships in the region. “If you look at Taiwan, Korea, China—the GEOs that we’ve been absent in—it kind of complements our GEO expansion,” said Narayan, noting that there were overlaps in Europe.

‘A competitive position in terms of power.’

Cyient Semiconductors is aggressively expanding in the power semiconductor space. During our interview, Narayan noted that the market stood at $44 billion. He sees Kinetic as a credible point of entry for the low-voltage space, while the company builds out its portfolio in the high-voltage and medium-voltage. “We’ll have all the pieces of the puzzle together over time: driver, controller, and GaN, or driver, controller, and FET.”

The power electronics industry has shifted a great deal of its spotlight to data center power delivery, from the grid down to the core, as demand for AI workloads soars. The near- to medium-term goal is to revamp the data center power infrastructure to eliminate all unnecessary losses, prompting data center operators to rethink power distribution. As a result, there is a massive demand and a subsequent vacuum for suitable data center power-conversion technology that silicon vendors are eyeing.

Data centers are one such use case that Cyient Semiconductors has its sights on as well, with a focus on conversion stages from 800 down to 6 V. “Our goal is to primarily focus on that front-end of the equation,” Narayan said.

He touched upon this during the above-referenced interview, addressing three major challenges in data centers:

  • Microsecond transient responses through fast-switching, digital control, and protection
  • Grid-to-grid power integrity with control architectures that monitor peak currents across AC and DC
  • Fault protection to prevent instability during power-collapse events

“On the low voltage side, we want to double down with the Kinetic team, who played in the protection side of things—voltage and current protection—both on the head end as well as on the protection side,” Narayan said. In other words, Kinetic can assist with this piece of the puzzle as well, with IP protection guarding against overvoltage events, surges, and fault conditions from the grid, as well as ongoing current and voltage monitoring at each conversion stage.

The short-term goal seems to be centered on ensuring that Kinetic generates enough revenue and profit to justify the purchase and any integration costs. “We’re driving to get the EPS [earnings per share] accretive by year two, so I think we’re positioning ourselves in the right direction in terms of being in a competitive position in power,” Narayan said.

Inside Cyient Semiconductors Bet on RISC-V for Custom Silicon

data.title: TSMC Unfolds Map for Process, Packaging Tech
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

TSMC just gave a peek at its chip roadmap for new process and packaging technology, including a photonics solution that promises to cut power consumption and reduce latency in AI data centers.

SVP Kevin Zhang briefed reporters and analysts a day before the opening of TSMC’s annual technology symposium, starting in California and continuing later this year around the world.

The company announced two derivatives of A14, its 1.4-nm, gate-all-around (GAA) process starting in 2028 that’s designed for AI data centers and edge devices. The A14 enhancements, A13 and A12, will begin production in 2029. A13 will reduce chip size by about 6% compared with A14, the company said.

TSMC also said N2U, a derivative of its world-leading 2-nm process, will start production in 2028, reducing power consumption by as much as 10%.

TSMC is trying to catch up with a surge in demand for AI chips that has helped surpass earlier expectations for chip production to reach $1 trillion by 2030. The semiconductor industry crossed the trillion-dollar threshold this year, Zhang said.

“By 2030, we’re saying the overall market revenue will exceed $1.5 trillion,” Zhang said. “The largest contribution will come from high-performance computing and AI, at over 55%.”

TSMC’s comments on advanced packaging, which integrates memory and logic chiplets into a single, high-performance package, were particularly noteworthy, according to Dan Hutcheson, vice chair at TechInsights.

Reviving Moore’s Law

“I saw their system-integration density gains as bringing life back to Moore’s Law,” Hutcheson told EE Times. “[Intel founder Gordon] Moore’s 1965 paper called for a doubling every year. That’s what TSMC clocked in at between 2024 and 2026. This, combined with the 6% lithographic density gain on A13, signals that advanced packaging is displacing lithography as the key enabler of density gains.”

For now, TSMC doesn’t need ASML’s most-advanced high-NA EUV lithography tools to make chips at the world-leading 2-nm and finer process nodes. Intel has adopted high-NA EUV equipment yet still lags TSMC in the advanced nodes.

“I’m amazed by our R&D team,” Zhang said. “They continue to find a way to drive technology scaling without using high-NA. One day, they may have to use it, but at this point, given the technology, with A14, A13, we continue to be able to harvest the benefits from current EUV, and not have to go to high-NA, which is very expensive.”

TSMC has been using its multi-patterning knowhow on its existing base of ASML low-NA tools as a workaround to make advanced-node chips ranging from 7 nm to 2 nm. One high-NA EUV tool costs about $380 million, according to Taiwan news source TrendForce.

“TSMC’s lack of a plan for high-NA EUV adoption was not a surprise,” Hutcheson said. “The issue they face is customer pushback on its field size being cut in half. It’s another signal that advanced packaging is displacing lithography as the key enabler of density gains. At least until the mask industry can develop a larger reticle.”

The size of a reticle limits the area that lithography tools can print in a single exposure. AI chipmakers are forced to overcome the reticle limit by making multiple chiplets that are combined in one 3D package.

One of TSMC’s disadvantages compared with rival Intel is its lack of backside power delivery in GAA chips. TSMC won’t start production of its super power rail tech until 2027 with the A16 process. Intel was first to bring backside power delivery into production in 2024.

Advanced packaging

Zhang showed a diagram of how TSMC sees the 3D architecture of chips evolving.

The diagram outlines TSMC’s new COUPE (Compact Universal Photonics Engine), entering production later this year, as well as a scheme to couple faster, higher density memory with compute logic.

This year, TSMC will roll out a 5.5 reticle-sized interposer it will use to integrate 12 HBM chips together with logic.

“Next year, we’re going to extend our CoWoS interposer size all the way to a 9.5× reticle, and in 2028, it will be 14×,” Zhang said.

As advanced packaging continues to evolve, the interposer can be a limitation, he noted.

“Ultimately, we’re going to use the so-called system-on-wafer (SOW) to bring more logic and compute units together,” he said. “You don’t need a substrate anymore. If you think about the whole wafer, a 300-mm wafer, it’s actually equivalent to a 40× reticle size when you measure by CoWoS or interposer technology.”

Cerebras is among the first to use SOW tech, making “somewhat unique” inferencing silicon, Zhang said.

“They already leveraged our wafer-level integration to bring so many compute die together,” Zhang added. “You basically bring the whole wafer as your base to integrate all the die. From a cost/performance perspective, it’s pretty competitive technology.”

CoWoS crunch ending

TSMC sees short supplies of its main advanced-packaging tech, CoWoS, ending soon.

“Our CoWoS capacity has grown substantially to reach a level to be able to address our customer demand at this point,” Zhang said.

TSMC will combine CoWoS with COUPE to bring the interposer closer to the switching die in AI chips.

“By doing that, you can achieve another 2× of latency reduction,” Zhang said. “At the same time, you actually improve power efficiency by about 2.5×. This is the ultimate integration technology we’re going to develop to provide better chip-to-chip communication in the future to address AI applications.”

TSMC follows smaller foundries like GlobalFoundries and Tower, which entered the silicon photonics business more than a year ago.

TSMC expects to continue 30% energy efficiency improvement per generation of chips.

“Every two years, we drive 30% efficiency,” he said.

Those efficiency improvements won’t necessarily be the same in the AI data centers that use TSMC chips, Zhang added.

“At the data center level, when you connect hundreds of thousands of GPUs or AI accelerators together, you need to have a lot of additional components,” Zhang told EE Times. “You have switches, power supply management, you have lots of things. The overall power benefit obviously has to be measured ultimately at the data-center level.”

The flurry of announcements from TSMC doesn’t necessarily signal that the company is accelerating the arrival of new process nodes, Hutcheson said.

“I’m not so sure that this is an acceleration of real new node introduction cadence as it is a willingness to be open and confident about releasing new nodes,” Hutcheson said. “The industry has had to deal with a lot of process complexity lately: GAA, backside power, new materials, in addition to simple scaling. I’d say they are getting this under more control and so are more confident about releasing their roadmap.”

Taiwan’s Tech Industry

TSMC 3-nm Upgrade in Japan to Catch up With Demand

data.title: Canada’s Defense Sector Talks Up Quantum
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Quantum technologies are foundational to modern defense and economic competitiveness, and Canada’s defense sector isn’t waiting for them to mature somewhere else in the world.

At a recent luncheon roundtable held at Canada’s Empire Club in downtown Toronto, the intersection of quantum and Canada’s defense sector was discussed by panelists representing industry and the country’s military.

“Allied nations are integrating quantum sensing, secure communications, and advanced computing into their core missions,” Saurabh Popat, chair of the Empire Club Board of Directors, said as he introduced the panel participants.

He said the shift from concept to capability is happening, as evidenced by North American Aerospace Defense Command (NORAD) modernization, NATO’s emerging quantum strategy, and Canada’s recently released Defense Industrial Strategy (DIS).

Defense will be the earliest adopter of quantum technologies because the advantages are too significant to ignore, Popat said. “If we do not convert Canada’s quantum strengths and advantage into deployable capability and industrial scale, they will be captured elsewhere along with the talent, IP, and economic value that will follow.”

Lisa Lambert, CEO of Quantum Industry Canada, said the DIS marks a significant shift in national policy, tightly linking sovereignty, security, and industrial capacity, with quantum technologies named as sovereign capabilities. “Within the strategy, quantum sensing, communications and computing are named as sovereign capabilities.”

She added that the word “quantum” appears 12 times in the document: “This is not accidental. It’s a demand signal, and demand signals carry obligations.”

Quantum will be an early indicator of how this broader shift unfolds regarding how Canada translates strategic intent into operational and industrial capacity, Lambert said. “Canada is moving from assuming security to engineering it, from observing industrial opportunity to building it, from inventing technologies to anchoring them.”

Although Canada boasts a globally leading quantum ecosystem, Lambert said it faces a persistent challenge of commercializing and retaining innovation domestically. With the right alignment, however, she said the country can capitalize on quantum for both defense and economic prosperity.

Lambert said continuous engagement among government, military, and industry is essential, as well strategic investment that mirrors efforts by allies such as the U.S. and the U.K., citing the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency’s (DARPA’s) Quantum Benchmarking Initiative, which has Canadian participants, and recently announced Canadian Quantum Champions Program.

Procurement must match technology maturity and support rapid deployment, Lambert said, and Canada is procuring quantum technologies today. “Quantum is inherently dual-use,” she said. “Many capabilities will be adopted first in defense because the missions demand it, but the value creation will extend across the entire economy.”

Lieutenant-General Darcy Molstad, the first Commander of Canadian Joint Forces Command (CJFC), which was formally established in late 2025, is tasked with engaging with industry to effectively leverage quantum and other advanced technologies. CJFC is a new organizational layer intended to make joint operations more coherent and responsive and better integrate capabilities such as cyber, space, intelligence, and multidomain operations under a single command structure.

Molstad said Canada’s new DIS demonstrates political will at all levels for collaborative engagement and a sense of urgency around operationalizing new capabilities and fostering industry-military partnerships. “We absolutely need the capabilities, and we need them yesterday,” he said.

Over the last two years, Molstad said there’s been a shift in the Canadian military’s approach to engaging with industry: “We have to work together on the problems and challenges.” He said it’s not realistic for him to write a statement of requirements defining military needs five years from now. “I need to tell you what my gaps are, what my challenges are, and we need to work together on those solutions and work together to operationalize them quickly.”

Molstad said a current focus for him is setting up a “rapid capabilities unit” within his organization that thinks and does things differently, including adding advisory capabilities on deep technology and venture capital.

He said quantum technologies for defense is an area where Canada can lead so it can demonstrate to allies that it is a net contributor, not a net consumer, of security and defense technologies. “We have to have our eyes wide open, but we have an opportunity to do some true nation building in every sector, not just quantum,” Molstad said. “We need moonshots.”

Quantum in Canada also needs significant amounts of external financing, said Francesco Bova, associate professor at the University of Toronto’s Rotman School of Management. “There’s other deep-tech domains where you could imagine bootstrapping your way to commercialization. We will not be bootstrapping our way to building a fault-tolerant quantum computer.”

Bova said raising capital in quantum is different than raising capital for other domains in the venture capital community because it often lacks recurring revenue. “Much of the domain is still within the R&D phase.”

David Roy-Guay, founder and CEO of SBQuantum, said a challenge for quantum startups like his is gaining access to real-world field deployments. The company has developed a novel quantum magnetometer that enables GPS-independent navigation in denied, degraded, or contested environments and by detecting magnetic anomalies associated with hidden objects and infrastructure.

Roy-Guay said the technology was designed in Canada but built elsewhere. He said it’s critical for SBQuantum and other companies to be able to deploy in realistic environments and gather feedback. “Once we demonstrate there’s really a problem to be solved there and that our technology can do better, then we can go around the supply chain and build the capabilities and do much more in Canada,” he said.

Roy-Guay’s advice for the emerging quantum companies is to start small and simple: “Get down to the end users, demonstrate what needs to be built, and once you have a big enough market, build it in-house.”

Don’t focus on perfection, he added. “That’s not going to make the difference for the end users. Cultivate your agility as you start so that you can deliver quicker than even the biggest companies all around the world.”

See also:

Optimism Drives Canada’s Semiconductor Scaling Efforts

Canada Eyes AI Opportunities at the Edge

data.title: EU DARE Project Is Scrambling to Replace Codasip
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The European High-Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (EuroHPC JU), an organization advancing European supercomputing, and the Digital Autonomy with RISC-V in Europe (DARE) consortium, an initiative backed by a €240 million budget, are actively managing the implications of a corporate realignment by Codasip, one of their project’s partners.

Munich-based Codasip recently announced a strategic pivot toward cyber-resilient architectures and the divestment of specific business units to an undisclosed U.S.-based public semiconductor company. This development introduces variables into the architectural and financial planning of the DARE project, which focuses on developing processors and accelerators for European data centers.

Codasip, DARE, and EuroHPC, contacted by EE Times, did not respond to requests for comments regarding the specifics of the divestment or the implications for the project. EE Times, however, collected reactions from other stakeholders across the ecosystem.

Codasip’s strategic realignment

Codasip is reallocating resources to address the rising demand for cyber-resilient SoCs. The company said digital infrastructure faces more threats, so security should be built into system architecture—not just added by software patches.

Consequently, Codasip is expanding its secure processor portfolio utilizing Capability Hardware Enhanced RISC Instructions (CHERI) technology. The objective is to deliver platforms built for cyber-resilience from the architecture outward, including field-programmable gate arrays and custom silicon. 

“Cyber-resilience has become a strategic requirement for governments, infrastructure operators, and technology providers worldwide,” said Ron Black, CEO of Codasip. “Traditional approaches inefficiently bolt security onto systems after the fact. Our focus is on enabling partners to build security into the fundamental architecture of compute systems from the beginning.”

In conjunction with this move, Codasip announced it is divesting its low-end RISC-V processor design business to an undisclosed U.S. public semiconductor company. As part of this transaction, the acquiring entity will also receive a broad license to Codasip Studio, an EDA tool used for the rapid customization of processor cores.

The transfer of the Codasip Studio EDA license to a U.S. company sparks discussions about how European-funded innovation and RISC-V intellectual property remain commercially available. Ecosystem leaders actively discuss how to secure long-term independence for the high-performance computing sector and ensure foundational design tools stay accessible to regional developers.

Architectural and roadmap considerations for DARE

“It is very concerning that a company such as Codasip has severe difficulties,” Mateo Valero Cortés, director of the Barcelona Supercomputing Center (BSC) and project coordinator, told EE Times. “Europe needs strong companies with leading technology to achieve true sovereignty.”  

Regarding the DARE project’s path forward, Valero indicated that stakeholders are proactively addressing the matter. “The EU and EuroHPC are aware of the situation and are looking for solutions and alternatives,” he said. “In my opinion, EuroHPC should find another European partner to take Codasip’s place.”

The DARE consortium relies on collaborative engineering to produce computing hardware, including a general-purpose processor (GPP) chiplet designed to interface with the OpenChip vector accelerator and the Axelera AI processing unit. Codasip’s shift away from high-performance, general-purpose design raises questions about how the consortium will mitigate architectural friction. The consortium must ensure that the GPP meets requirements for data throughput and low-latency memory access to prevent bottlenecks for the associated accelerators.

Furthermore, introducing a new GPP architecture or modifying the design could lead to roadmap adjustments, including changes to the software co-design efforts led by the BSC and the Jülich Supercomputing Centre. The team must also consider financial and restructuring elements within the €240 million DARE SGA1 project budget.

The consortium must address costs for restructuring the grant agreement, potentially onboarding a replacement GPP partner, and ensuring overall financial stability.

The DARE consortium maintains a measured outlook. In a recent partner update, the organization confirmed ongoing dialogue with Codasip and EuroHPC JU to manage the transition. DARE reiterated its commitment to project goals and noted that all activities continue as planned while it evaluates next steps and manages the transition within the project.

Investment realities and the ecosystem response

The challenges faced by semiconductor companies often stem from the funding environment. During a press briefing on April 15 in Barcelona, Cesc Guim, CEO of Barcelona-based OpenChip, a spinoff of the BSC and a partner in the DARE project, shared insights on the structural differences between European and American investment cultures. He observed that European investors often scrutinize immediate cash flow, which is difficult for semiconductor startups to demonstrate given the multi-year development cycles of custom chips.

In contrast, capital in the U.S. is often deployed more rapidly, driven by foundational ideas and technological potential. Guim noted that if Europe intends to cultivate semiconductor companies, investment processes must become faster, more agile, and less risk-averse.

Guim expressed empathy for Codasip’s situation. “It’s sad for Europe that that’s happening to Codasip, to be honest,” Guim stated. “We try to help them as much as we can in that… try to see how we can work together.”

OpenChip, which began with gap funding from the European Commission and has grown to employ over 300 people, focuses on building a credible business plan backed by early customer engagement. Guim detailed that technology companies must focus heavily on business acumen to ensure financial viability, rather than relying solely on technical merit.

To foster a more robust environment for chip designers, Guim advocates mechanisms from European governing bodies that quickly match private investments with public funds to mitigate the risk-averse nature of regional investors.

Furthermore, Roger Espasa, CEO of Semidynamics, highlighted the fundamental need for unified action among European companies during another press briefing. Asked about Codasip, he argued that many companies fail because they lack fundamental business skills in both the private and public sectors. He also mentioned that Codasip is fundamentally an EDA company, and he feels that Europe needs more players in that field.

Codasip’s strategic divestment and operational pivot introduce variables for the DARE consortium, but its partners said they are seeking alternatives to demonstrate the ecosystem’s resilience.

The shared commitment to cultivating RISC-V intellectual property and secure processing capabilities forms a central pillar of Europe’s technological framework. Ongoing discussions among consortium members and regional alliances show their collective focus on long-term stability.

See also:

European Project Gets $260 Million for HPC Chip Sovereignty

Is Europe Securing the Wrong Chips?

Codasip: Toward Custom, Safe, Secure RISC-V Compute Cores

data.title: First Take on CadenceLive and Its AI Agent Stacks for EDA
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The centerpiece of Cadence Design Systems’ announcements at its annual user conference in Santa Clara, California, was a hierarchical agentic AI EDA stack for system design, comprising a head orchestrator plus domain-specific “super agents” to coordinate across engineering domains and accelerate the EDA design process.

You can get a summary of what that means in this video interview with Rob Knoth, senior director responsible for strategy and new ventures at Cadence. In this discussion with EE Times, Knoth explains the implications, and we also touch on the question of business model—how EDA companies such as Cadence will charge for AI agents and virtual engineers.

In the second video, Ian Cutress, chief analyst at More Than Moore, offers his perspective on the announcements made at CadenceLive.

Audio files are available below for listening.

Rob Knoth at CadenceLive: AI Agent Stacks To Scale and Democratize Chip and System Design

Ian Cutress, Nitin Dahad discuss CadenceLive 2026

More coverage from CadenceLive will follow next week.

data.title: TSMC Chases Soaring AI Demand
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

TSMC is boosting spending plans as it chases soaring AI demand and outpaces its chipmaking rivals.

The world’s largest chip foundry this week said it expects to spend nearly $56 billion this year as it “invests heavily” in production capacity to meet demand from AI customers such as Nvidia, AMD, and Apple. Even at that increased level of spending, TSMC didn’t deny that it will still probably fall short of demand in 2027.

“It takes two to three years to build a new fab, no shortcuts,” CEO C.C. Wei said on a conference call this week to announce first-quarter 2026 results. “It takes another one to two years to ramp it up.”

The company said it will rush construction of three new fabs in Japan, Taiwan, and the U.S. to make 3-nm chips. The Taiwan facility will start production in the first half of 2027, followed by the U.S. site in late 2027 and Japan in 2028.

The company said that, for the first time, it will expand 3-nm production, a node that’s been established for several years. It’s a process technology where the company has no rivals.

“Historically, we do not add additional capacity to a node once it has reached its target capacity,” Wei said. “We are now executing a global capacity plan to support the robust multi-year pipeline of demand for 3-nm technologies, which are used by smartphones, HPC AI—including HBM-based dies—automotive, and IoT customers.”

TSMC started production of the world’s most advanced 2-nm chips late last year, leading competitors such as Samsung and Intel. Still, TSMC’s rivals are sharpening their edge, according to Handel Jones, CEO at International Business Strategies.

“TSMC will continue to be the leader in technology and market share, but the performance of Samsung has improved significantly,” Jones told EE Times.

The large financial resources of Samsung’s memory and logic businesses, in addition to its branded electronics operations, provide an advantage of scale. Profits in the memory business this year are soaring.

“The memory revenues of Samsung will be over $200 billion in 2026, and operating income will be $120 billion or more,” Jones predicted.

Samsung is also catching up in process technology.

“Samsung’s SF2P [second-generation 2 nm] is becoming competitive with N2 [TSMC’s 2-nm tech] in power, performance, and area,” Jones said. “Samsung has obtained a number of high-volume customers and will be capacity-limited.”

Samsung, the world’s second-ranked foundry, will probably more than double its production capacity in Austin, Texas, to 50,000 wafers per month, Jones predicted. The company is adding another 20,000 wafers per month in South Korea and increasing its internal consumption of 2-nm wafers, he said.

Still, TSMC’s rivals won’t take back market share in any meaningful way until 2028, according to Sravan Kundojjala, an analyst at SemiAnalysis.

“TSMC has a more-than five-year head start on N3/N2 yield that competitors cannot compress,” Kundojjala told EE Times. “TSMC’s foundry revenue share went from 54% in 2020 to 72% in 2026, and the gap is still widening.”

Samsung stumbled at the 3-nm node more than two years ago. Intel, revamping a year after new CEO Lip-Bu Tan took the helm, could take some business off TSMC’s hands in advanced packaging. TSMC doesn’t rule out the possibility of opening its compute die to competitors such as Intel. There’s plenty of business to share, Wei said.

“We understand that our competitors also offer very attractive technology, but we welcome that so our customers can have more choices,” Wei said on the conference call. “Then, we can do more business with our customers.”

Intel has very good opportunities in packaging, Jones said.

Intel’s EMIB for advanced packaging is a real technology, but the question is ecosystem and volume, Kundojjala said.

“TSMC holds over 50% of CoWoS capacity through Nvidia alone,” he said. “The challenge for Intel is that customers want front-end wafers and back-end packaging from the same vendor for yield co-optimization. Splitting the two creates integration risk.”

TSMC is adopting new tech for panel-level packaging, which replaces round silicon wafers with square panels. The company is building a pilot line for its CoPoS [Chip-on-Panel-on-Substrate] tech that will probably start production in a few years, Wei said. CoPoS helps increase the density of large AI chiplets and HBM stacks that exceed reticle limits.

ASE, TSMC’s packaging partner, said earlier this year it’s ramping up panel-level packaging for the first time.

Shortages

Shortages of advanced technology wafers and packaging will persist through 2030, according to Jones.

“In 2030, demand for 2 nm and below will be 400K to 450K wafers per month,” he predicted. “Capacity will be 300K to 350K wafers per month.”

TSMC said it will start production of its newest node, A14, in 2028. Compared with TSMC’s 2 nm, A14 will provide up to 15% speed improvement at the same power or up to 30% power improvement at the same speed and close to 20% chip density gain, the company claimed.

“Our A14 technology and its derivatives will further extend our technology leadership position and enable TSMC to capture the growth opportunities well into the future,” Wei said.

In addition to cleanroom construction, several constraints limit TSMC’s ability to increase production, according to Kundojjala.

“ASML’s scarce EUV capacity now has multiple customers,” he said. “Non-EUV tools from Applied Materials, Lam, KLA, and TEL are also capacity-constrained, though lead times are shorter compared to EUV. The Middle East situation is creating price pressure on helium, neon, and specialty chemicals, and Taiwan’s LNG supply chain is being stress-tested. Of these three, equipment supply is the binding constraint through 2027.”

Musk’s Terafab

Wei brushed off the plan of Elon Musk to build TeraFab, a semiconductor complex in Texas, backed by Musk’s Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI, targeting new capacity for 2-nm logic, memory, advanced packaging, and a mask shop in record time on a tight budget.

“Intel and Tesla, they are TSMC’s customers, but again, they are our competitors, and we view Intel as our formidable competitor,” Wei said. “The fundamental rules of the foundry game never change. They need the technology leadership, manufacturing excellence, and customer trust, and most of all, the service.”

See also:

Three Misconceptions About the $402B Semiconductor Foundry Industry

The Silicon Hegemon

data.title: YMTC’s NAND Design Surprise Alongside a New Fab
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

At a time when the U.S. trade sanctions aim to hamper China’s fabs from manufacturing cutting-edge logic and memory chips by restricting the supply of the latest fabrication equipment, materials, and tools, the news about Chinese chipmaker Yangtze Memory Technologies Corp. (YMTC) building new fabs comes with an element of surprise.

What makes it even more intriguing is the current memory chip crunch sparked by an unprecedented demand in what pundits call the golden era of AI infrastructure. Will this window of opportunity bring YMTC to the league of Kioxia, Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix? Industry analysts are closely watching this memory resurgence from China at a critical technology juncture.

And they wonder how a Chinese chipmaker is building new fabs while facing sanctions on the supply of the latest fabrication equipment from major international firms and churning out advanced NAND flash chips that compete with global heavyweights such as Kioxia and Samsung.

South Korean media outlet ChosunBiz has reported that YMTC is set to start mass production of cutting‑edge NAND products at a new fab in Wuhan, Hubei province, in the second half of 2026. In addition to this new fab, known as Phase 3, YMTC plans to build two more fabs, each producing 100,000 wafers per month.

What has startled the industry watchers about YMTC’s upcoming fab, aiming to produce 50,000 wafers by 2027, is that more than 50% of Phase 3’s equipment, materials, and tools have been sourced from Chinese suppliers. That includes tools for vertical stacking of 3D NAND layers.

YMTC’s first two fabs, Phase 1 and Phase 2, were largely built around Western fabrication equipment before the 2022 U.S. restrictions, which halted access to lithography, etching, deposition, and cleaning processes from major suppliers such as ASML and Applied Materials. Since then, YMTC has been working closely with local suppliers like Advanced Micro-Fabrication Equipment (AMEC), a fabrication equipment company that offers etching and MOCVD equipment.

NAND flash underdog

YMTC, China’s first mass producer of 3D NAND flash memory chips, is now rapidly narrowing the gap with market leaders Kioxia, Micron, Samsung, and SK Hynix. In 2025, YMTC accounted for 11.8% of the $52 billion global NAND flash market, while market leader Samsung commanded ⁠30.4%. SK Hynix, Kioxia, and Micron held 16%, 15.9%, and 13.3%, respectively.

Earlier this year, YMTC unveiled the new Xtacking 4.0 memory chip design for its 3D NAND chips; it implements two separate dies for logic and memory array. That led to the creation of the 64L 3D NAND flash device, which represents the first major competitive semiconductor product from YMTC.

The Xtacking 4.0 architecture runs at 270 layers, behind SK Hynix’s 4D NAND flash at 321 layers and Samsung’s 9th-gen V-NAND at 286 layers. In Xtacking architecture, periphery circuits and memory cell operations are processed on a separate wafer. So, the array efficiency and memory bit density are considerably higher than conventional 3D NAND chips such as Samsung’s 64L V-NAND and Kioxia’s 64L BiCS NAND.

However, it’s important to note that YMTC’s NAND flash device consumption mainly comes from China’s smartphone, notebook, and server companies, which benefit from government subsidies for using local chips. Take the case of Acer, Adata, Lexar, and Team Group, which employ YMTC’s NAND flash chips in their SSDs and other storage devices.

Though, according to TechInsights, YMTC also offers a 10-20% price edge over Japanese, Korean, and U.S. rivals. “Chinese manufacturers often enjoy a price advantage of more than 15% for products of the same specifications,” said Arisa Liu, chief director and research fellow at Taiwan Industry Economics Services, a unit of the Taiwan Institute of Economic Research. “It’s highly attractive to the price-sensitive general-purpose server and consumer markets.”

Still, YMTC’s technological know-how and rapid strides in NAND flash are a testament to China’s pursuit of semiconductor self-sufficiency. While quality and security concerns continue to restrict memory adoption outside China, underdogs such as YMTC, offering price advantage paired with rising yields and more mature processes, could eventually shift procurement decisions in the global memory markets.

The DRAM push

Industry reports suggest that YMTC has decided to allocate around 50% of Phase 3’s capacity to DRAM rather than NAND. And that it’s working on through-silicon via (TSV)-based packaging for stacking DRAMs in high-bandwidth memory (HBM), widely used alongside AI accelerators.

There are also reports that YMTC has sent low-power double data rate (LPDDR) samples to clients and expects feedback by the end of the year. That, in turn, is likely to shape the DRAM production capacity that YMTC allocates across its new fabs.

It’s worth noting that ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT), the other major memory chipmaker in China, is already eying high-end DRAM technology for AI data centers. CXMT is shifting 20% of its DRAM capacity to HBM3 while it aims to complete HBM3 mass-production certification later this year. Next, HBM3E chips are expected to start shipping within two years.

The rise of two memory chipmakers in China despite U.S. trade sanctions marks a defining moment in China’s drive for self-sufficiency, as the global memory chip market faces dramatic shortages. More importantly, Chinese memory chipmakers are building new fabs while sourcing fabrication equipment, materials, and tools from local companies that offer lithography, etching, deposition, and cleaning processes.

See also:

China’s Memory Ambitions Yield Competitive NAND

U.S. Blacklists YMTC, 21 Chinese Companies on AI Threat

YMTC’s Lead Over Samsung, Micron in 3D NAND Challenged

data.title: Positron AI Enters Nvidia Turf With Oracle Deal
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

AI chip designer Positron was among a few to tread on Nvidia’s turf in AI data centers when the startup sold its first chips to Oracle this year, Positron CEO Mitesh Agrawal told EE Times in an interview.

“We are actually deploying multiple tens of millions of dollars’ worth of systems and racks into their Oracle cloud infrastructure for running inference workloads, especially targeted for mixture-of-experts [MOE]-modeled inference setups,” Agrawal said. “That’s one of the primary use cases.”

Outside of big names, such as Nvidia, AMD, and Google, startup Positron is one of about 10 chip designers aiming to enter the processor business for AI data centers.

In April, Intel and SambaNova formed a multiyear partnership aimed at AI inference. The effort is focused on delivering high-performance, cost-efficient inference for agentic AI, targeting enterprise and cloud providers in the second half of 2026.

Business consultant Deloitte projects inference will account for roughly two-thirds of AI compute workloads by 2026, doubling from 2023 to a market worth $50 billion.

Majestic Labs and Positron are developing new silicon to address the memory wall that limits more affordable inference processing, especially for larger models that exceed the memory capacity of startups such as Groq and Cerebras, Karl Freund, principal analyst at Cambrian-AI Research, told EE Times.

“The latter companies do quite well for agentic AI and MOE, but inferencing on larger models and/or large contexts over 500 billion parameters demands a new architecture to deliver massive capacity and bandwidth,” Freund said.  “Venture capital is flowing to this new generation of startups,” he noted.

Fundraising

Among nine inference startups raising capital this year, the largest amounts went to South Korea’s Rebellions, with $400 million in March, and Cerebras, garnering $1 billion in February, according to a March report from PitchBook.

Instead of inference and training being “either-or,” it more likely will be “both-and,” according to professional services provider Deloitte in a 2025 report. There’s likely to be considerable growth in inference-only or inference-optimized chips, but at the same time, the kind of chips typically best suited for foundational model pre-training, post-training, and test-time scaling, which are a mix of training and inference compute, remain the powerful, energy-hungry GPUs with HBM that cost tens of thousands of dollars each, Deloitte added.

Customers

“Outside of Nvidia and AMD, we are the first AI silicon company to get actually deployed in the hyperscaler cloud,” Positron’s Agrawal said. “Tens of millions of dollars is not that much for these cloud providers; they’re spending tens of billions of dollars. It’s just a start for us.”

In addition to Oracle, Positron has also won an investor, Jump Trading, as a customer.

“There’s a lot of other customer domains in that sector, so that’ll be very interesting for us to grow into,” Agrawal said. “We’ve had some other proof-of-concept customers like [cybersecurity provider] Cloudflare, like [technology infrastructure company] Crusoe, but as we grow and build this, hopefully we can convert a few of them into large-scale revenue customers just like Oracle as well.”

Positron also aims to enter existing data centers that need to maximize efficiency.

“We have a couple of customers right now in the pipeline that could range anywhere between a $10 million deal structure to [low] tens of millions [of dollars’ worth] of deployment structure,” Agrawal said. “One of the critical driving factors there is the fact that they have power, but that power is at lower-power rack density. Each rack is anywhere between 15 to 30 kilowatts, and they’re air-cooled, so they can’t deploy your [liquid-cooled] Blackwells and Rubins of the world. They want to monetize the power that they have available. The reason that they’re going to buy from us is because we are proving out that we can generate tokens at a competitive rate, but it is also as much about utilizing that empty power.”

Asimov chip

Positron’s Asimov chip, set to enter the market in the middle of next year, will be positioned to “co-exist” with Nvidia’s Rubin line of GPUs.

“Asimov continues to stay air-cooled,” Agrawal said. “We are driving toward around 450- to 500-watt thermal design power per chip for a per-system deployment of 3 kilowatts. We aim to stay air-cooled and continue to drive that data center bottleneck story.”

Nvidia owns the world in terms of training and inference, Agrawal noted.

“It’s more about co-existing, finding new use cases, finding the leftover power,” he said.

“Each megawatt is tens of millions of revenue for us.”

Positron would be “lucky” to capture 0.1% of the $50 billion GPU market, Agrawal noted.

Nvidia, Google, AMD, and AWS have effectively captured the GPU business for data centers, he added.

“That basically rounds out 99.9% of the market,” Agrawal said. “Then you have a little bit of Cerebras, a little bit of Positron, maybe a little SambaNova, and that’s pretty much it for AI silicon.”

Positron’s Asimov will be made on TSMC’s N3P node, which the world’s leading foundry brought to production in late 2024.

“One of the interesting things that we’re doing is we’re not using CoWoS plus HBM,” Agrawal said. “We’re using organic substrate plus DRAM as attached memory to the chip. We’re using LPDDR memory, which is commodity memory, so prices have gone up, but allocation is there. We’ve solved the supply chain problem of how to get memory.”

Positron’s chiplet-based approach combines modules of LPDDR so that the aggregate memory bandwidth gets closer to that of HBM, he noted.

Asimov’s other key advantage from an architectural perspective is that the memory bandwidth utilization (MBU) is over 90%, according to Agrawal.

“The best-in-class MBU that you’ll see for an inference workload is in a range of 20% to 45%, or maybe 50%,” Agrawal said. “Even though you can get a top-line number from HBM4 on Nvidia Rubin of like 16 terabytes per second, your usable memory bandwidth is anywhere between 2.5 to 8 terabytes per second at most. We only have 11 terabytes per second, but we can use 93% of that available MBU.”

No allocation at TSMC Arizona

Positron can’t get allocation for production of Asimov at TSMC Arizona, one of three sites in the U.S. that Agrawal had been aiming for. Positron made its first chips as an FPGA implementation at Intel Foundry in the U.S.

“[TSMC Arizona] has been completely booked out by major players like Apple and Nvidia,” Agrawal said. “In 2027, obviously, we will rely on TSMC’s good graces. If they can move us to Arizona, we’d love it. It’ll be then fully U.S.-based manufacturing. Based on our conversations right now, we think it’ll be Taiwan for all our production in 2027. In 2028, when Apple moves on to 2 nm and AMD moves on to 2 nm, we can start dreaming about getting allocation in Arizona for 3 nm. Arizona will be much more scaled up by that time.”

TSMC declined to comment.

See also:

Startup Positron Takes On Nvidia With FPGAs

Positron on Role of Arm CPU in Orchestration of Intelligence

data.title: Apple Eyes Product Engineering Revival with CEO Transition
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Tim Cook, a supply chain genius, is passing the baton at Apple to hardware engineer John Ternus, who led the development of chips that replaced Intel processors in Mac computers. As the head of hardware engineering at Apple, Ternus also spearheaded the development of Apple Watch, AirPods, and Vision Pro headsets.

Ternus, currently Apple’s senior VP of hardware engineering, will take over the CEO job on Sept. 1, while Cook assumes the role of executive chairman, replacing longtime chair Art Levinson. Cook is known to have been working on his succession for nearly a year.

What does this CEO transition signal? For a start, while Cook has been widely acknowledged as a smooth operator, taking Apple from a $350 billion company in 2011 to nearly a $4 trillion market cap in 2026, he wasn’t a product CEO. Cook squeezed so much profit from the device business that Apple became the world’s most valuable company, but critics feel that Apple lost its innovation streak during his 15-year tenure.

Ternus, an affable engineer who rose through the ranks of Apple’s hardware division, has spent his 25-year tenure building the company’s most consequential products: from iPhone to iPad to AirPods. So, his ascent to the leadership role marks a shift back to product-led innovation at Apple.

Next, and equally crucial, is the fact that Apple is probably the only tech behemoth perceived as having missed the AI boat over the past decade. It’s important to note that Apple was the first smartphone maker to capture public imagination by incorporating Siri virtual assistant into the iPhone back in 2011.

Fast forward to 2026, industry reports suggest that Apple has turned to Google to launch an updated Siri powered by the Gemini model. One of the first challenges awaiting Ternus will be executing a promised revamp of the Siri virtual assistant and thus making Apple relevant in the AI world again. It’d be interesting to watch how he integrates AI into the iPhone, the most successful consumer product in history.

Ternus and the silicon frontier

Another factor prominent in this transition is Apple’s in-house silicon development. One of Ternus’ major accomplishments has been working with Apple’s in-house silicon team to replace Intel processors in Mac computers with Apple-designed chips. The chips proved more energy efficient, and as a result, Mac sales soared after this processor change.

Case in point: Johny Srouji, who currently oversees Apple’s custom chip and sensor designs, has been named chief hardware officer. Srouji, who worked at Intel from 1990 to 2005, joined Apple in 2008 to lead the development of the A4 chip, Apple’s first system-on-chip (SoC) device. Eventually, he helped Apple build one of the world’s most innovative teams of silicon engineers.

Apple’s in-house silicon also enabled the company to build a MacBook Air with a thinner profile, 18 hours of battery life, and performance comparable to a MacBook Pro. Here, Ternus was a major influence in bringing critical technologies in-house for such design undertakings.

He also played a key role in switching over to the M1 chip in iPad Pro. He made the case that the M1 chip used in the iPad Pro offers the same performance as the Mac counterparts. In M1, the 8-core CPU design featured faster performance in low-power silicon, while the 8-core GPU delivered faster graphics.

Cook, the opposite of Steve Jobs’ charismatic, feisty leadership style, brought calm execution and operational discipline while building one of the most efficient technology ecosystems in corporate history. On the other hand, Ternus is well-known inside Apple for his engineering competence. Cook praised him for having the mind of an engineer, the soul of an innovator, and the heart to lead with integrity and honor.

While Cook was an operations guru, Ternus’ tenure is likely to bring a renewed focus on engineering-centric product innovation to launch new devices such as folding phones, smart glasses, VR devices, and AI pins. Meanwhile, Apple will have to get its AI act together.

See also:

Probing the Apple M1’s Hidden Depths

Apple Elevates Supply Chain Under Cook

Apple’s A4 Dissected, Discussed…and Tantalizing

data.title: Tenstorrent Previews Large Compute Cluster, Generates Video Faster Than Real Time
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Ahead of a full launch of Tenstorrent’s new generation of cluster-scale systems next week, EE Times got a first look at the company’s video generation demo. The Wan2.2-14B-based demo generated a five-second video (720p, 81 frames, 40 steps, based on an optimized model) from a text prompt in three seconds when EE Times tried it out. The company said its record of 2.4 seconds is around 10× faster than the same production-grade model running on other leading hardware.

The demo runs an optimized version of Wan2.2-14B created by partner Prodia, a fast image and video generation AI cloud company. It runs on four new BlackHole-generation Galaxy servers (128 Tenstorrent BlackHole chips).

Tenstorrent’s hardware architecture for AI unifies compute, memory, and networking to enable very large systems to run a single software program. This is achieved without proprietary interconnects or reconfiguration, and isn’t workload-specific.

While there’s a lot of focus on code generation today, video generation will take off imminently and drive entire markets, Tenstorrent senior fellow Jasmina Vasiljevic told EE Times.

“We’re pretty excited about video,” Vasiljevic said. “We see that with more Galaxies, we can tackle bigger resolutions and longer videos.”

One of the reasons video applications had stalled is that they required too much compute, she said.

“It used to take many minutes to generate a single video; now we can produce five seconds of video in two and a half seconds, which is better than real time,” she said. “Breaking the real-time barrier will unlock new verticals.”

A big bottleneck for video generation inference today is iterative denoising, which can be parallelized across frames but is ultimately a sequential process. New research is exploring autoregressive or predictive models that predict future tokens or frames similarly to how text LLMs make predictions, which is less sequential in nature. Tenstorrent sees the future of video generation as a combination of both approaches.

“The likelihood of these algorithms evolving aggressively to combine is very high,” Vasiljevic said. “We’re excited because [Tenstorrent] is good at both.”

Video customers

Tenstorrent CEO Jim Keller told EE Times that the company is working with some video-focused customers, including some of the big frontier AI labs.

“We’re pretty sure we’re the only ones who’ve been able to do [generation of video] at this kind of speed, and at these cost numbers,” he said. “Our mission right now is just to keep making it easier to do and easier to deploy, but the actual models run pretty solidly and are stable.”

Many video customers are running minor tweaks or iterations of core models, he said, which can run on top of the work Tenstorrent has already done on models such as Wan2.2-14B.

“The engine underneath is pretty common, which is cool because it makes porting relatively straightforward,” he said. “The big diffusion models have been around for a while, and everybody knows how they work, but running them across 128 chips in parallel at high utilization is new.”

Common ground across today’s video generation models means customers can get up and running quickly, especially given Tenstorrent’s fully open-source software stack. But the real aim is for the architecture to be general-purpose enough to handle the next generation of models, too, Keller said.

“Our mission has always been general-purpose AI computing… we’re anti-specialized,” he said. “With a lot of chips, we have a lot of SRAM, but all the chips also have DRAM, and they are highly networked together, so our platform is more general-purpose. The world is talking about specialized, specialized, specialized, and that should be terrifying [everyone] because as models change, that specialized hardware is not going to work.”

Benchmarks for other workloads are expected at Tenstorrent’s at-scale compute launch next week.

  • We updated the article on 22/4/26 after Tenstorrent contacted us to clarify the amount of chips needed to run the video generation demo.

data.title: Indian Researchers Develop Molecular Memristor for Neuromorphic Computing
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The Centre for Nano Science and Engineering (CeNSE) at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc), in Bangalore, led by professor Navakanta Bhat and associate professor Sreetosh Goswami, has developed a molecular memristor based on a ruthenium compound that is claimed to deliver 14-bit analog resolution and 4.1 TOPS per watt (TOPS/W), enabling linear weight updates and improved energy efficiency for neuromorphic computing in signal processing applications.

In simple terms, the researchers said they have built a molecule that behaves like a tiny, power-sipping neuron synapse, and then arranged millions of them in a grid to perform AI calculations the way a human brain does, at a fraction of the energy.

The ruthenium complex uses azo-aromatic ligands (carefully engineered organic rings) that exhibit zeroth-order kinetics, meaning that the switching happens at a constant, predictable rate, regardless of conditions. This results in a 14-bit resolution and linear, symmetric conductance updates across four orders of magnitude.

The researchers said the approach could support neuromorphic computing for edge AI applications, where power and latency constraints limit the use of conventional GPU-based systems.

In an interview with EE Times, IIsc Bangalore professors Bhat and Goswami said they are developing a prototype through a startup incubated at CeNSE. The chip is expected within the next year and is being designed on a 22-nm process using a crossbar architecture that performs computation within memory.

Identifying the need

Current AI systems are limited by energy use, with about 94.00% of convolutional neural network (CNN) energy consumed in dynamic random-access memory (DRAM) access rather than computation. Conventional chips calculate AI workloads step by step, and the number of steps increases as models get bigger.

Semiconductor industry expert and former COO of MIPS Ashok Balivada said, “You need high performance for edge AI, but you also have to keep power consumption low. Existing approaches are largely incremental. Digital technologies rely on careful implementation to manage power. There are in-memory and near-memory compute approaches, but they are still evolutionary.”

Intel’s Loihi is an experimental neuromorphic computer chip for spike-based neural networks. Digital AI accelerators such as Groq’s LPU, now acquired by Nvidia, and IBM’s NorthPole prioritize fast, energy-efficient inference using large on-chip digital designs, though NorthPole draws some inspiration from brain architecture. IIT Bombay is focused on assembling and wiring memristors reliably at scale, while IISc Bangalore is focused on engineering a better memristor material from the ground up.

Balivada said the current research at IIsc Bangalore combines three directions: new materials, analog computation, and brain-inspired architectures. “At its core, it performs matrix multiplication and related operations efficiently,” he said. “The power advantage comes from analog techniques.” He added that the design must still integrate into standard CMOS flows, with the base system remaining digital.

Goswami said the research stems from limits in existing computing models and questions assumptions at multiple levels, from materials to architecture, while remaining compatible with silicon-based manufacturing. Bhat added that the group is incubating a startup at CeNSE and building a team to commercialize the technology. “We believe that within the next year we will have a minimum viable product ready,” he said. The planned tape-out is on a 22-nm TSMC process.

How the neuromorphic chip functions

The chip under development is designed as an AI inference accelerator. Balivada said the effort has two main goals: building the molecular memristor in a monolithic form and integrating it into a mixed-signal SoC. “Future products must be monolithic because discrete implementations may not meet cost and other requirements,” he said.

He added that the chip includes analog front-end design, digital data paths, and on-chip static random-access memory (SRAM). The system is being designed to run workloads such as image classification, object detection, and voice-based interfaces. “This device will function as an AI accelerator connected to a host such as a laptop or desktop,” he said.

Neuromorphic chips use a crossbar grid in which voltages and currents perform matrix computations physically and simultaneously, collapsing what would otherwise scale quadratically into a single parallel operation. Bhat explained that the system performs a multiply-accumulate (MAC) operation using basic electrical principles. “The matrix is stored as conductance, and the input is applied as voltage,” he said. “The product of voltage and conductance produces current, which represents multiplication.” Currents from multiple cells sum along the wires according to Kirchhoff’s current law, thereby completing the accumulation step. “What would require thousands of digital MAC operations happens instantaneously,” he said.

Goswami added that system design must balance analog and digital operations. “Even if 99% of AI computation, which is dominated by vector-matrix multiplication, is accelerated, the remaining 1% can still consume substantial power,” he said. The IISc team is working on interfaces between analog computation and digital control, higher on-chip memory integration, and co-design of algorithms and hardware to handle analog noise and lower precision.

Physical architecture of the chip

Unlike many existing memory technologies, the material does not rely on defects. “If the system is defect-based, the process becomes stochastic,” Goswami said. “For analog, you do not want randomness. Our material is deterministic. You can program it where it ought to be.”

The molecular film is about 60 nm thick, roughly a thousand times thinner than a human hair. At the atomic level, Goswami said the system relies on ion movement between molecular positions. “Each configuration gives a different level of electron transport,” he said. “These are stable memory states.”

The device can be scaled in area from one micrometer to tens of nanometers. Bhat said this scaling increases density without changing thickness, which remains about 70 nm. Goswami added that even at smaller sizes, the device maintains usable precision. “From 14-bit, at around 50 nm, we still get about 11 bits,” he said. “For 8-bit inference, this is not a problem.”

The team of researchers said ruthenium is available easily and is not a supply constraint. They are also exploring alternatives based on iron and cobalt. “There is a research pipeline for such materials,” Goswami said.

On the reliability of the memristor’s stored states, the team reported stable operation over months. “We have tested programmed states after seven to eight months, and there is no difference,” Goswami said. He added that endurance has been tested up to 10^9 cycles, compared to about 10^6 cycles for flash memory.

Bhat said conductance remains stable with standard passivation techniques used in CMOS manufacturing. “Once you store a conductance, it stays put for long periods,” he said.

The chip design is currently targeting 8-bit resolution, with options for 4-bit operations to reduce power. Data converter speeds are about 100 megasamples per second, with throughput scaling through parallelism.

The road ahead

Balivada said the design is currently in the RTL design phase. The team is using tools from Synopsys, Cadence, and Siemens. Some components are being reused, while others, such as data converters, are under development. Workload validation is being carried out using frameworks such as PyTorch and TensorFlow.

The technology is protected through multiple patents. “The material forms the base, and we also have patents at the architecture and system level,” Bhat said. The startup will license these from IISc.

On industry engagement, Balivada said the team plans to provide chips for testing once available. Partnerships are expected with system developers, customers, and universities. The group is also working with the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) on specific applications.

While challenges remain in integration and access to design intellectual property, the team said there are no fundamental barriers. “We have a clear path,” Bhat said. “It is about execution.” Balivada added that integration into commercial fabrication flows is feasible. “This operates in the back end of the line and does not interfere with front-end transistor processes,” he said.

Neuromorphic computing may still be a niche market, valued at about $213 million in 2025, but is projected to grow more than sevenfold to roughly $1.6 billion by 2034, according to a Fortune Business Insights report, as demand for brain-inspired AI architectures gathers momentum. The research team at IISc Bangalore is focusing on SoC integration for edge AI inference, including scaling crossbar arrays and reducing device dimensions. Medium-term work includes moving toward more analog operation and higher-density on-chip nonvolatile memory. Longer-term goals include enabling on-chip training with backpropagation.

Read also:

Rain Neuromorphics Tapes Out Demo Chip for Analog AI

Innatera Taps VLSI Expert to Lower Barriers to Neuromorphic Adoption

EU Project Looks to Mimic Biological Neural-System Processing

data.title: Game, Set, Bot: Sony AI’s ‘Ace’ Serves Up a Defeat to Table Tennis Pros
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Sony AI, launched in April 2020, has developed an autonomous robot designed to compete with human table tennis players, marking a step forward in robotics and physical AI.

Codenamed “Ace,” Sony AI claims the system represents the first instance of a robot reaching human, expert-level performance in a competitive physical sport. The work builds on the company’s AI agent, Gran Turismo Sophy, first announced in March last year, and reflects its use of gaming environments as benchmarks for testing an AI agent to model behavior and actions at the fastest time scales humans can achieve.

“In the history of AI, games have played a crucial role as benchmarks,” Peter Dürr, director of Sony AI in Zürich, told EE Times in an interview. “The reason for this is that in games that humans play, you can easily compare the performance of the AI with human performance.”

According to Dürr, gaming offers a useful testbed for pushing the boundaries of today’s software because it can faithfully replicate real-world scenarios that would otherwise be difficult—or unsafe—to recreate in real life.

“Going from the virtual world to the real world… that step is actually really hard for several reasons,” Dürr said. “The first one, maybe the most obvious one, is that in Gran Turismo, for example, if our racing AI makes a mistake and there is a big crash, the drivers are still safe behind the screen… you can push a button, and the cars are good as new. In the real world, with a real table tennis robot as fast as ours, that’s actually very dangerous. In the worst case, we could hit the table, or the robot could hit itself, or even something could fly off and injure the human opponent. So, we have to be really, really careful with that.”

A key factor in Sony AI’s approach, according to Dürr, is the use of a virtual gaming environment to run reinforcement learning at large scale over short timeframes.

“Fundamentally, what you have to understand is we are not programming the robot in the classical sense,” Dürr explained. “Because if you try to program a robot that plays table tennis, you don’t realize there’s so many different situations the robot can be in that it becomes impossible to write the code by hand. What we do is use learning and we train the robot in simulation. What you see is basically the result of thousands of hours of simulated table tennis… no one knows how this robot should hit the ball best, but we can let it do a random motion, and […] once in a while, it will hit the ball in a way that makes it go back to the table. Then we have what we call a reward signal where we can say, ‘Okay, that was good, do that kind of stuff again.’ And by doing this over and over and over again… the algorithm learns how to return the ball better and better, and we reward it for hitting the ball faster, and we reward it for hitting the ball with more spin, so it becomes stronger and stronger at playing table tennis.”

However, this approach also introduces technology limitations. Although simulations can approximate real-world conditions, they can’t fully anticipate all physical variables, including factors such as racket bounce and other contact dynamics.

To address these gaps, Sony AI said it has combined a perception system, an AI agent, and robotic hardware.

Precision, control, and hardware

One of the most difficult challenges in developing Ace, Dürr said, was not only anticipating snap decisions made by elite and professional table tennis players during a match, but also tracking a ball that can travel at 20 to 30 meters per second in the air while spinning at more than 160 rotations per second.

“In the history of robotics, there have been many research teams that [have tried] to solve robot table tennis,” he said. “One aspect that has historically been very difficult is to understand the spin of the ball as it flies through the air. It’s massive amount of spin that’s imparted on the ball… no one could measure in real time accurately the spin of the table tennis ball. So, we set out to solve that.”

Sony AI said it has addressed this challenge by equipping Ace with three core components: precision, control, and hardware.

Precision

Ace relies on a total of nine active pixel sensor (APS) cameras to triangulate the ball in 3D space at 200 Hz. To measure angular velocity (spin), the robot employs three gaze control systems (GCSs) that track the ball using event-based vision sensors, tunable lenses, and pan/tilt mirrors.

Control

Ace uses deep reinforcement learning policies trained in simulation via an asymmetric actor-critic architecture. The policy generates actions at 31.25 Hz (every 32 ms), which are mapped to trajectory segments for the robot. A model predictive control planner ensures these movements are collision-free.

Hardware

Sony AI said it has developed a custom-built platform that features eight degrees of freedom (two prismatic and six revolute joints) to achieve the necessary speed and agility to compete against elite and professional players.

Real-world results

In April 2025, the research team put Ace to the test against a total of five elite and two professional table tennis players. Out of the five elite players, Ace won three matches that totaled seven games won out of 13 games played, according to the research paper. Out of the two professional players, Ace lost both matches, winning one game out of the seven games played.

During these games, the team extrapolated data that showed Ace successfully achieved a return rate above 75%, suggesting Ace won points through consistent returns, not faster shots.

Looking beyond sports

Though Ace was built specifically to play table tennis, the underlying research could inform broader advances in physical AI.

“The success of [Ace], enabled by the new perception system and new learning-based control algorithm… suggests that similar techniques apply to other areas featuring fast, real-time control and human interaction, including, for example, manufacturing and service robotics,” the team said in its research report.

Dürr also expressed his optimism that the research could help the broader robotics community develop physical AI agents capable of performing complex, real-time interactive tasks in a wide range of use cases.

“In my opinion as a researcher, there are kind of a two classes of approaches,” he said. “One is to try to design a system that’s very, very general and can solve many, many different tasks. The other is what we did. Basically, we used a very general method that can be applied to all kinds of tasks… but we only train it on table tennis, and we try to really get to the maximum performance. There is a certain convergence where we come together with these two types of approaches, and I think that’s what’s going to be very interesting to see over the next four to five years.”
